summary: Gruber defends AppleScript against its reputation for being hard to write, arguing the difficulty stems from inconsistent application scripting dictionaries rather than the language itself. He highlights the 'whose' clause as an elegant feature that allows filtered references without explicit loops. Through practical examples using BBEdit and the Finder, he demonstrates how 'whose' clauses can filter collections of objects declaratively. The post serves as a brief tutorial teasing a follow-up where these clauses will be put to practical use.
key_points: AppleScript's difficulty reputation comes from inconsistent app scripting dictionaries, not the core language
The 'whose' clause enables declarative filtered references, eliminating the need for manual loops
Filtered references work across any well-scripted application, from BBEdit to the Finder
AppleScript's core language is actually small and offers elegant features when understood properly
money_quotes: AppleScript gets a rap for being easy-to-read, but hard-to-write. And it's a somewhat deserving reputation.
But the problem isn't with the language itself, per se, but rather with the syntactical vagaries of disparate application scripting dictionaries.
The AppleScript language itself is rather small, and in fact offers some very neat features.
key_insight: AppleScript's real elegance lies in features like 'whose' clauses that allow declarative filtering, and its notorious difficulty is better blamed on inconsistent application dictionaries than on the language itself.
